choices.text: Three problems if you don't: (1) Performance — filtering on unindexed columns causes full table scans; one consumer adding `?filter=description=...` on a 10M-row table takes the database down, (2) Security — clients can filter on internal fields you didn't mean to expose (`internal_score`, `admin_notes`, `is_test_account`); enumeration via filter values leaks info, (3) Predictability — supported queries should be documented and stable; arbitrary filter combinations are an unbounded surface area. Allowlist pattern: define `ALLOWED_FILTERS = {'status', 'team', 'created_at'}` at the endpoint; reject anything else with 400. When you add a new filter, add it intentionally + add the corresponding index. Code review catches accidental exposures. Same applies to sort fields (ORDER BY on unindexed column is also a scan), include parameters (which related resources can be eagerly loaded), and any user-controlled query shape. Allowlists turn 'oh no the API now does anything' into 'API does exactly these things, documented.'

questions.explanation: Performance (only indexed columns), security (no internal field exposure), predictability (documented surface).

questions.prompt: Users want free-text search on `?q=...`. You're using Postgres with `LIKE '%aaron%'`. Concern?

choices.text: Slow + bad relevance. `LIKE '%term%'` can't use a btree index (leading wildcard makes it useless); on tables larger than tens of thousands of rows, it scans linearly — 100k rows = hundreds of ms to seconds per query. Plus: no relevance ranking (returns matches in random order), no tokenization (searching 'admin' won't match 'administrator'), no fuzzy matching for typos, no synonym handling. Postgres alternatives that work: full-text search with `tsvector`/`tsquery` + GIN index (fast, handles tokenization and ranking, built into Postgres — try it first); trigram extension (`pg_trgm`) for typo-tolerant fuzzy matching. For more serious search: a dedicated search engine — Elasticsearch / OpenSearch / Meilisearch / Algolia / Typesense. They handle relevance, faceting, autocomplete, multilingual stemming. Pattern: write data to your database for storage; index for search in the search engine; search queries hit the search engine. Most apps don't need this until they're past hello-world scale, but `LIKE '%x%'` doesn't scale past small tables and produces inferior UX.

questions.explanation: LIKE '%x%' can't use index — full scan. Use Postgres FTS (tsvector + GIN) or a search engine for real search.
